chunk_summary: Dialysis-specific nutrition in pediatric CKD requires tailored plans for PD and HD, addressing energy, protein, and micronutrient needs while managing treatment-day challenges and growth outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of dialysis modalities (PD and HD) on energy and protein requirements in pediatric CKD.
Develop dialysis-specific nutrition plans to support growth and prevent malnutrition in children.
Identify strategies to manage energy intake, protein losses, and intradialytic symptoms in PD and HD.
Recognize the role of IDPN in malnourished children on maintenance HD.
key_facts: In PD, glucose absorbed from dialysate contributes unprescribed calories, potentially leading to excess fat gain with poor linear growth.
PD causes consistent protein losses, often greater in younger children, requiring adjustments toward the upper protein range during high losses or peritonitis.
In HD, intradialytic symptoms like nausea and hypotension suppress intake; a pre-dialysis small meal and post-dialysis snack are recommended.
Monitor height velocity and MUAC/TSF to assess energy delivery; reduce energy cautiously if weight gain accelerates without height response.
IDPN is reserved for malnourished children on maintenance HD who cannot meet needs orally or via tube feeding.
reference_quote: The dietary management of energy and protein in children with CKD stages 2–5 and on dialysis—clinical practice recommendations from the Pediatric Renal Nutrition Taskforce.
document_key_topics: Dialysis-specific nutrition differences in PD and HD
Energy and protein needs in pediatric CKD
Growth monitoring and malnutrition prevention in dialysis
Role of IDPN in pediatric HD patients
